author: MelodyHunter_Pro
title: Discover Weekly recommendations are becoming stale and repetitive
body: I've been a loyal Spotify Premium subscriber for 4 years and I feel compelled to write this post because the decline in Discover Weekly quality has become impossible to ignore. Three weeks ago I got a Discover Weekly playlist with 18 songs I'd already heard. Last week, 14 songs were from artists already in my library. This week, 11 songs were explicitly in my 'Liked Songs'. The feature is supposed to discover NEW music for me. It's fundamentally not doing that anymore. Has anyone at Spotify actually listened to what their algorithm is producing?

author: RadioRepeatSufferer
title: Artist radio keeps looping the same 30 songs
body: When I turn on Artist Radio for any artist, it plays the same 30-40 songs in roughly the same order every single time. I've tested this by keeping a log for 3 months. The variation between sessions is under 15%. This is not a radio - it's a very slow shuffle of a fixed playlist. Actual radio introduces you to new music. This feature should be renamed 'Artist Shuffle' because calling it a 'Radio' implies discovery that simply doesn't happen.

author: CasualListener_Jane
title: Can we please get a 'reset my recommendations' button?
body: I've wanted to try different genres but I'm afraid of ruining my current recommendations. Right now the algorithm has me perfectly pegged for mellow indie music. If I explore hip-hop or country for a week, I know from past experience that it'll contaminate my recommendations for months. Why isn't there simply a 'reset recommendations' or 'explore mode' that doesn't affect my core taste profile? This seems like a basic UX feature that would solve so many problems.

author: DailyMix_Disappointed
title: Daily Mix has literally not changed in 3 months
body: I am not exaggerating. I have screenshotted my Daily Mix 1 every Monday for the past 3 months. The core 15 songs are identical across all 12 weeks. Only 2-3 songs rotate in and out at the edges. How is this called 'Daily' when it doesn't actually change daily? More importantly, how is this a 'Mix' when it's the same fixed set? I feel misled by the feature name. Spotify should either fix the feature or rename it to 'Static Comfort Playlist'.

author: FreeUserVoice
title: Free tier discovery feels deliberately limited
body: I'm on the free tier and the recommendation quality feels deliberately constrained. My premium-using friends show me their Discover Weekly and it's genuinely diverse. Mine defaults to top 50 global hits after every listening session. I understand premium gets better features, but shouldn't discovery be a baseline feature for everyone? Discovery helps users find more music they love, which keeps them on the platform. Limiting it on free tier seems self-defeating.

author: EchoChamberExperience
title: Trapped in an indie-folk echo chamber and can't escape
body: Six months ago I went through a breakup and listened to a lot of sad indie-folk music. I've recovered emotionally but Spotify has not. Every single discovery feature - Discover Weekly, Daily Mix, Radio, Made For You - all serve me indie-folk sad music exclusively. I've tried: skipping every indie-folk song, creating playlists with happy music, explicitly playing artists outside this genre. Nothing works. The echo chamber is self-reinforcing because even my attempts to escape it (skipping) just signal that I 'didn't like that specific song' not that I want a different genre.

author: WorkdayStreamer
title: Focus music recommendations are stuck in a loop
body: I use Spotify all day at work for focus music. I've played lo-fi hip hop and ambient music constantly for the past year. Now I literally cannot escape it in recommendations. Even when I want to explore something energetic or upbeat, the algorithm keeps pulling me back to focus music because that's 90% of my playcount. The algorithm needs to understand that high playcount = habitual use, not necessarily strong preference.

author: GlobalMusicFan
title: Non-English music discovery is essentially broken
body: I listen to music in 6 different languages. Spotify's algorithm cannot process this. When I switch between listening to Arabic music, Japanese city pop, and English rock, the algorithm creates bizarre hybrid recommendations that don't serve any of my actual preferences. There seems to be no understanding that language preference and sonic preference are different dimensions. The algorithm needs to understand music across language barriers, not just within language silos.

author: PowerUser_Playlists
title: Algorithm ignores my 5 years of curated playlists
body: I have spent 5 years building playlists that capture distinct musical moods, genres, and moments. 127 playlists with over 8,000 songs total. You'd think Spotify would have incredible insight into my taste from this data. Instead, my Discover Weekly recommends mainstream pop hits that I would never add to any of my playlists. The algorithm isn't reading my playlist curation signals at all - it only looks at what I actively play, ignoring the massive signal contained in what I've chosen to curate.

author: TrialUserFeedback
title: New user - algorithm locked me in after 1 week of trial
body: I just finished my 3-month Premium trial and wanted to share feedback. In week 1, I explored 8 different genres trying to figure out what Spotify had to offer. By week 2, my recommendations had completely locked onto one of those genres and never let go. For weeks 2-12, I was stuck in a rock echo chamber even though I'd only spent 20% of week 1 listening to rock. The onboarding exploration phase should be clearly marked as separate from regular recommendation building.

author: LongTimeFan_2015
title: Remember when Discover Weekly was actually exciting?
body: I've been using Spotify since 2014 and Discover Weekly since it launched in 2015. In 2015-2017, it was genuinely the best thing in music technology. It felt like magic - it knew my taste and still surprised me. Today it feels like a mediocre shuffle of my existing library. I genuinely believe something changed in the algorithm around 2021-2022 that made it much more conservative. Whatever optimization target they changed to, it wasn't 'genuine discovery'.

author: FamilyPlanMember
title: Family plan recommendation contamination is real
body: Our family of 5 shares a Family Plan but we each have separate accounts. Despite separate accounts, I can clearly see cross-contamination in recommendations. When my teenager listens to K-pop on their account, K-pop starts appearing in my recommendations the next day. We're on the same WiFi network and family plan - Spotify seems to be sharing taste data across accounts linked to the same plan. This is a serious privacy concern and ruins recommendation quality.

author: NicheGenreSeeker
title: Discover Weekly has never correctly identified my niche genre
body: I listen to progressive death metal, specifically technical and avant-garde subgenres. Spotify's Discover Weekly has never - in 4 years - correctly identified this preference. It gives me generic metal or popular death metal bands that everyone knows. The long tail of niche genre discovery is completely inaccessible. Bandcamp and last.fm's recommendation systems do a significantly better job with niche tastes because they rely on community tagging rather than purely algorithmic inference.

author: AutoplayHater
title: Autoplay after playlist should not be a fixed 20-song rotation
body: Every time any of my playlists ends, autoplay activates and plays essentially the same 20 songs. I've mapped this out - the autoplay pool seems to be around 50 songs total but only 20 are played per session. This is out of a catalog of 100 million songs. The algorithm is clearly being extremely conservative with autoplay to avoid churn or skip. But the result is I hear the same songs so often I've started actively disliking them. Over-familiarity is breeding contempt.

author: ContextualListenerPro
title: Spotify needs contextual listening modes desperately
body: My ideal Spotify would have: Commute Mode, Work Mode, Workout Mode, Wind-Down Mode. Each mode would build its own recommendation profile based on what I listen to in that context. Right now, my 7am high-energy commute music is contaminating my 10pm wind-down recommendations. And vice versa. Context-aware recommendation is a solved technical problem - I know because I work in ML. Spotify just hasn't prioritized building it. This single feature would completely transform the discovery experience.

author: StudentBudget
title: Student discount but still feels like second-class discovery
body: I'm on the student premium plan - it's the same premium features, right? But my friends on regular premium say their discovery is noticeably better. Maybe it's coincidence but there seems to be a difference in recommendation quality between student and regular premium. My Discover Weekly is heavily weighted toward whatever's trending on TikTok at any given moment. I'd rather have genuine taste-based recommendations than viral recommendations.

author: HeadphoneAudiophile
title: Too many promoted songs in Discover Weekly killing genuine discovery
body: I've started noticing a pattern in my Discover Weekly: approximately 4-6 songs per week seem to be 'promoted' tracks - new releases from major label artists that don't match my taste profile. These are likely paid placements or editorial pushes from Spotify's deals with labels. Each promoted song that isn't genuinely relevant to my taste is a wasted slot that could have been a genuine discovery. Please, no advertising in Discover Weekly. Keep it pure.

author: RetroMusicLover
title: Algorithm has no concept of temporal discovery - all recommendations are 'safe'
body: What I mean: I discovered Radiohead in 1995 when it was new and exciting. If the algorithm was serving me in 1995, it would have recommended them as a discovery. Now in 2024, recommending Radiohead to a new user is 'safe' but not discovery - it's just serving mainstream choices. The algorithm should understand that 'new to the USER' is more important than 'objectively new'. A 1970s folk song I've never heard is a discovery for me, even if it's 50 years old.

author: BothPlatformsUser
title: Apple Music's radio is genuinely better at discovery - controversial opinion
body: I'm going to say something controversial in a Spotify community: Apple Music Radio (specifically the genre-based radio stations) is significantly better at genuine discovery. I use both platforms. Spotify Radio plays the same 40 songs. Apple Music Radio has genuinely surfaced artists I'd never encountered. The difference is Spotify's radio is algorithmically conservative while Apple's mixes algorithm with editorial. Human curation still matters for discovery.

author: ActiveMusicExplorer
title: I actively want to explore but the UX makes it painful
body: I AM trying to explore new music. Every week I try to deliberately listen to new genres and artists. But the UX fights me at every step. There's no way to tell the algorithm 'I'm in exploration mode'. There's no easy way to mark something as 'I listened to this to explore, not because I love it'. Skipping signals dislike. Not skipping signals approval. There's no 'meh, interesting experiment' signal. The binary like/dislike model is too crude for nuanced taste exploration.
